insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution. A cryptographically verified receipt settles at roughly $0.10 — the same confirmation event, 50–200x cheaper, with no lookback window or pixel dependency.

With Chrome deprecation joining Safari and Firefox, the cross-site tracking stack is effectively gone. Verified receipt data is identity-free and requires no cookie infrastructure — it was built for the environment that now exists, not adapted to it.

The Home Depot's appearance in top spend signals that the dataset has crossed from SaaS-only into physical retail. For CPG and home improvement brands, that's the first evidence this signal reaches brick-and-mortar purchase events.

Active markets expanded from 13 to 21 CBSAs in four reporting cycles, with non-metro penetration now visible in FL, WI, TN, ME, and NV — organic spread with no paid acquisition, suggesting the spend categories attract receipts from mainstream consumer geographies, not just early-adopter clusters.

Identity-free receipt data carries no GDPR/CCPA consent burden and no breach liability — the attributes that make identity-attached data expensive to hold are simply absent. Clean-room and Privacy Sandbox workarounds exist because brands need what this design already provides.

insights.topic: Vendor queue as category demand signal
insights.insight: Five domains are queued for approval — Notion and Vercel are the highest-signal entries because both generate recurring monthly invoices, not one-time events. Independent agents surfacing the same gap without coordination is a reliable leading indicator of which SaaS categories will dominate the next spend cohort.
